subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome back to the world of numbers to 100! You already know how to count these numbers and split them into tens and ones. In this unit you will use place value in some exciting new ways. You will learn to write numbers in words, to round a number to the nearest ten, and to regroup a number into many different shapes. Then you will discover how to find a fraction of a number – how to take a half or a quarter of a whole group of things.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 2 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Read and write two-digit numbers in words, using the hyphen correctly.
Round a two-digit number to the nearest 10.
Use a number line to help you round and to picture numbers.
Regroup a two-digit number in several different ways using tens and ones.
Understand that finding a fraction of a number means dividing into equal parts.
Find halves of numbers by dividing by 2, and quarters by dividing by 4.
Recognise that ½ and ²⁄₄ are equivalent fractions – they name the same amount.
Work backward from a fraction to find the whole number.
Solve fair-sharing word problems using fractions and division.

concepts.title: Reading and Writing Numbers in Words
concepts.explanation: Sometimes numbers are written not with digits but with words. You see number words on notices, in stories, on prices and in letters. To read and write them, you need to know the special words for each part of a number. The good news is that once you learn a small set of words, you can build every two-digit number up to 100. First, the tens words. These name the whole tens: 10 is ten, 20 is twenty, 30 is thirty, 40 is forty, 50 is fifty, 60 is sixty, 70 is seventy, 80 is eighty, and 90 is ninety. Notice that forty…
concepts.example: Next, the numbers from 11 to 19 have their own special names that you simply have to learn: eleven, twelve, thirteen, fourteen, fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen and nineteen. They do not follow the neat pattern of…

concepts.title: Rounding to the Nearest 10
concepts.explanation: Rounding helps us work with simpler numbers. When a number is a little awkward, we can change it to the nearest ten to make it easier to think about. Rounding does not give the exact answer – it gives a close, tidy answer that is quick to use. When Halima glances at 58 shillings and thinks ‘that is about 60’, she has rounded to the nearest ten. To round a two-digit number to the nearest 10, you look at just one digit: the ones digit. Here is the rule:
concepts.example: Look at the ones digit of the number.

concepts.title: Regrouping Numbers in Different Ways
concepts.explanation: Regrouping means breaking a number into tens and ones in a new way. You already know the standard way to split a number: 31 is 3 tens and 1 one. But that is not the only way to make 31! We can trade one whole ten for ten ones and still have exactly the same amount. Regrouping is like giving change: swapping one ten-shilling coin for ten one-shilling coins does not change how much money you have. Let us regroup the number 31 in every way we can:
concepts.example: 3 tens and 1 one (this is the standard way): 30 + 1 = 31.

concepts.title: Fractions of Numbers – Finding Halves
concepts.explanation: You have already learned about fractions of shapes – cutting a circle of anjero into equal parts. Now you will learn about fractions of numbers, which means sharing a whole group of things into equal parts. A fraction of a number means dividing the number into equal parts and taking some of those parts. Two special words help us here. The denominator is the bottom number of a fraction, and it tells us how many equal parts to divide into. The numerator is the top number, and it tells us how many of those parts to…
concepts.example: To find half of a number, written ½, we divide the number by 2. Finding a half and dividing by two are exactly the same thing, because a half means one of two equal parts. Look at these examples:

concepts.title: Finding Quarters of Numbers
concepts.explanation: A quarter, written ¼, is one of four equal parts. To find a quarter of a number, we divide the number by 4, because the denominator 4 tells us to split into four equal groups. Finding a quarter and dividing by four are the same thing. Picture this as sharing equally between 4 groups. When Hassan and his three brothers – four boys in all – share a bag of 8 apples in Kismaayo, they deal the apples into four equal piles and each boy gets 2. So a quarter of 8 is 2. Notice that each brother received one of the four…
concepts.example: There is a clever link between halves and quarters. A quarter is half of a half. To find a quarter of 20, you could first halve it to get 10, and then halve again to get 5. Finding ¼ of 20 by dividing by 4, and halving…

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions – ½ and ²⁄₄
concepts.explanation: Some fractions look different but have exactly the same value. We call these equivalent fractions. The most important pair for you to know is ½ and ²⁄₄. These two fractions name the same amount, even though one is written with a 2 on the bottom and the other with a 4. Let us prove it with a number. Find ½ of 8: that is 8 ÷ 2 = 4. Now find ²⁄₄ of 8: first divide 8 into four equal parts (8 ÷ 4 = 2 in each part), then take two of those parts (2 + 2 = 4). Both answers are 4! So ½ of 8 and ²⁄₄ of 8 give the same…
concepts.example: You can also see this with a shape. Take a square of paper. If you divide it into 2 equal parts and shade 1 part, you have shaded ½. Now take an identical square, divide it into 4 equal parts, and shade 2 parts – you…

methods.title: How to write a number in words
steps: Split the number into tens and ones.
Write the tens word (twenty, thirty, forty …).
Add a hyphen, then write the ones word.
Check special cases: numbers 11–19 have their own names.

methods.title: How to round to the nearest 10
steps: Look at the ones digit only.
If it is 0, 1, 2, 3 or 4, round down to the lower ten.
If it is 5, 6, 7, 8 or 9, round up to the higher ten.

methods.title: How to find a fraction of a number
steps: Read the denominator – it tells how many equal parts.
Divide the number by the denominator.
For a half divide by 2; for a quarter divide by 4.
To find the whole from a fraction, multiply by the denominator instead.

methods.title: Round a halfway number
steps: Solution: The ones digit is 5, which sits exactly halfway.
The rule says a five always rounds up, so 45 rounds to 50.
Check the round a halfway number result carefully.

methods.title: Regroup a number
methods.example: Find three different ways to regroup 45 using tens and ones.
steps: Solution: Start with the standard way, 4 tens and 5 ones.
Trade one ten for ten ones to get 3 tens and 15 ones.
Trade again to get 2 tens and 25 ones.
Each version still equals 45, because 40 + 5 = 30 + 15 = 20 + 25 = 45.

methods.title: Find half of a number
methods.example: Halima has 18 dates. She gives half to Sahra. How many dates does she give away?
steps: Solution: Half means divide by 2.
So Halima gives away 9 dates.
Check by doubling: 9 × 2 = 18.

workedExamples.title: Write a number in words
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Split 68 into tens and ones: 6 tens and 8 ones. Six tens is sixty. The ones part is eight. Join them with a hyphen: sixty-eight. Do not forget the hyphen.

workedExamples.title: Read a number word
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The word ‘seventy’ means 7 tens = 70. The word ‘two’ means 2 ones. Put them together: 72. The tens word always comes first.

workedExamples.title: Round to the nearest 10
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look at the ones digit of 58, which is 8. Because 8 is 5 or more, we round up. The ten above 58 is 60, so 58 rounds to 60.

workedExamples.title: Round a halfway number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The ones digit is 5, which sits exactly halfway. The rule says a five always rounds up, so 45 rounds to 50.

workedExamples.title: Regroup a number
workedExamples.prompt: Find three different ways to regroup 45 using tens and ones.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start with the standard way, 4 tens and 5 ones. Trade one ten for ten ones to get 3 tens and 15 ones. Trade again to get 2 tens and 25 ones. Each version still equals 45, because 40 + 5 = 30 + 15 = 20 + 25 = 45.

workedExamples.title: Find half of a number
workedExamples.prompt: Halima has 18 dates. She gives half to Sahra. How many dates does she give away?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Half means divide by 2. ½ of 18 = 18 ÷ 2 = 9. So Halima gives away 9 dates. Check by doubling: 9 × 2 = 18. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Find a quarter of a number
workedExamples.prompt: A bag holds 20 marbles. Abdi gives a quarter of them to his brother. How many marbles is that?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A quarter means divide by 4. ¼ of 20 = 20 ÷ 4 = 5. So Abdi gives 5 marbles. Check by halving twice: half of 20 is 10, and half of 10 is 5. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Work backward from a fraction
workedExamples.prompt: A quarter of a number is 6. What is the number?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: To find the whole from a quarter, multiply by the denominator, which is 4. So the number is 6 × 4 = 24. Check: ¼ of 24 = 24 ÷ 4 = 6. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Find three different ways to regroup 45 using tens and ones.
workedExamples.solution: 4 tens 5 ones; 3 tens 15 ones; 2 tens 25 ones (also 1 ten 35 ones, 0 tens 45 ones).

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: Find three different ways to regroup 62 using tens and ones.
workedExamples.solution: 6 tens 2 ones; 5 tens 12 ones; 4 tens 22 ones (also 3 tens 32 ones, 2 tens 42 ones, 1 ten 52 ones, 0 tens 62 ones).

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Complete these equivalent fractions: a) ½ of 8 = ___ and ²⁄₄ of 8 = ___ b) ½ of 20 = ___ and ²⁄₄ of 20 = ___.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: Half of a number is 5. What is the number? Show how you checked.

activities.title: Number Word Cards
activities.materials: small pieces of paper, a pencil.
steps: On some cards write two-digit numbers as digits (like 24, 57, 83).
On matching cards write the same numbers in words (twenty-four, fifty-seven, eighty-three).
Shuffle all the cards and turn them face down.
Play a matching game: turn over two cards and keep them if the digits and the words match. Check that every word card has the correct hyphen.
Watch out for the tricky spelling of forty – it has no ‘u’!

activities.title: Rounding on a Homemade Number Line
activities.materials: a long strip of paper, a ruler, a pencil.
steps: Draw a number line and mark the tens: 0, 10, 20, 30, all the way to 100.
Ask a family member to call out a two-digit number, like 47 or 72.
Find the number on your line and decide which ten it is closest to.
Say the rounded number out loud, then check with the ones-digit rule: 0 to 4 rounds down, 5 to 9 rounds up.

activities.title: Bundles and Regrouping (Stick Trading)
activities.materials: 31 small sticks, stones or dry beans, and some string or rubber bands.
steps: Tie your objects into bundles of ten. You should have 3 bundles and 1 loose one.
Say the standard regrouping: ‘3 tens and 1 one’.
Now untie one bundle and lay out ten loose ones. Say the new regrouping: ‘2 tens and 11 ones’.
Keep untying bundles and say each new way, until you reach ‘0 tens and 31 ones’. The pile never changes size!

activities.title: The Fair-Sharing Investigation
activities.materials: a handful of dates, stones or bottle caps, and some small bowls or plates.
steps: Count out 20 objects. Share them equally into 2 bowls to find half. How many in each bowl?
Now put them all back and share them equally into 4 bowls to find a quarter. How many in each bowl?
Notice that a quarter is half of a half. Check: half of 20 is 10, and half of 10 is 5.
Try again with 12 objects, then 16 objects. Record your halves and quarters in a little table.

activities.title: Equivalent Fraction Squares (Family Game)
activities.materials: two equal squares of paper, colouring pencils.
steps: Fold the first square into 2 equal parts and colour 1 part. This shows ½.
Fold the second square into 4 equal parts and colour 2 parts. This shows ²⁄₄.
Place the two squares side by side. Is the same amount coloured on each?
Show a family member how ½ and ²⁄₄ cover the same amount, proving they are equivalent.

activities.title: Rounding Market Game
activities.materials: paper price tags, stones as pretend shillings.
steps: Set up a pretend market with items priced at numbers like 23, 58, 45 and 74 shillings.
Before ‘buying’, round each price to the nearest 10 to guess roughly how much you will spend.
Add up your rounded prices to make a quick estimate.
Then add the exact prices and compare. Was your rounded estimate close?

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand halves and quarters best? What was the trickiest part of rounding or regrouping? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Numbers in Words Rule
rules.text: Write the tens word first, then the ones word, joined by a hyphen. 47 = forty-seven. Remember: forty has no letter u.

rules.title: Rounding Rule
rules.text: Look only at the ones digit to round to the nearest ten. 0 to 4 rounds down. 5 to 9 rounds up. A five always rounds up.

rules.title: Fractions Rule
rules.text: Finding a fraction of a number means dividing into equal parts. Half means divide by 2. Quarter means divide by 4.

terms: A number written in letters, like forty-two
The dash joining the tens word to the ones word
Change a number to the nearest ten
The multiple of ten a number is closest to
Break a number into tens and ones a new way
One of two equal parts; divide by 2
One of four equal parts; divide by 4
Bottom number / top number of a fraction
Fractions with the same value, like ½ and ²⁄₄
commonMistakes: Look only at the ones digit

selfAssessment: I can read and write two-digit numbers in words, using the hyphen correctly.
I can round a two-digit number to the nearest 10.
I can use a number line to help you round and to picture numbers.
I can regroup a two-digit number in several different ways using tens and ones.
I can understand that finding a fraction of a number means dividing into equal parts.
I can find halves of numbers by dividing by 2, and quarters by dividing by 4.
I can recognise that ½ and ²⁄₄ are equivalent fractions – they name the same amount.
I can work backward from a fraction to find the whole number.
